Q1: I tried to keep things pretty structured, honestly. I would set aside specific blocks during the week for studying, usually in the evenings after my extracurricular activities. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often listen to lecture recordings or talk through concepts out loud to myself. Even though I didn't make it to class that often, I made sure to put in about 22 hours a week at home going through the material systematically.
Q2: I tried to stick to regular times, like Tuesday and Thursday evenings and a longer session on Sundays. But I'll admit that when deadlines got close, everything would shift and I'd cram more into those final days. The routine helped me feel in control, but it wasn't always possible to maintain it perfectly when life got hectic.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew there was something due or when I needed to check announcements. I'd also go in to watch the lecture videos since I missed a lot of classes in person. Sometimes I'd just log in out of habit to make sure I wasn't missing anything important, even if I didn't stay long.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks it became automatic to check the LMS every couple of days. I think it happened because I realized early on that missing announcements could really hurt me. So logging in became part of my evening routine, almost like checking my phone for messages. It felt safer to stay on top of things that way.
Q5: The video lectures were by far the most useful for me because I learn best by listening. I could replay sections I didn't understand and pause to take notes. The discussion forums were also helpful sometimes because hearing other students' questions made me realize things I hadn't thought about. Those two resources really carried me through the course.
Q6: I mostly ignored the extra reading materials and some of the optional quizzes. I just didn't have time for everything, and I figured if it wasn't required, I could skip it. The readings especially felt redundant when I'd already watched the video covering the same topic. I had to prioritize what would actually count toward my grade.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was clearly connected to an assignment or exam. If I could see how it would help me complete something that was being graded, I was all in. I also valued things that I could listen to rather than just read, since that matches how I learn best. Anything that felt like busywork got pushed aside.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—they felt like extras I couldn't afford to spend time on. Required ones had this weight to them, like I knew I had to do them or face consequences. With optional stuff, I'd tell myself I'd get to it later, but later never really came. The pressure of required work always won out.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that wouldn't show up anywhere in the LMS. I'd download the audio from lectures and listen to them on my phone while commuting or doing chores. I also had a notebook where I wrote out key concepts and would review those pages repeatedly. That kind of studying was invisible but really important for me.
Q10: There were plenty of times when I was studying intensely but the LMS wouldn't know. Like when I'd spend an hour talking through concepts with a friend over the phone, or when I'd record myself explaining topics and play it back. I also used some YouTube videos to supplement what we learned. None of that would show up in any log.
Q11: Yes, definitely. I'd often download a lecture video or save the slides, and then I wouldn't need to go back online. I'd study from those files for days without touching the LMS again. It was more convenient for me, especially when my internet was being slow or I was somewhere without good connection.
Q12: There were moments when fear kind of paralyzed me. I'd feel scared about falling behind or not understanding something, and instead of pushing through, I'd freeze up. My attendance was already low, so sometimes I felt like I was already too far behind to catch up. Those moments were really hard, and I'd have to push myself to re-engage.
Q13: Fatigue was a big factor for me. Between my extracurriculars and trying to maintain 22 hours of study, I'd sometimes be exhausted. When I was tired, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities definitely made me cut corners on some LMS activities too.
Q14: Yeah, there were times when I'd have the video playing but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd be stressed or scared about something, and even though I was technically watching the content, nothing was sticking. I'd have to go back and rewatch things later when I was in a better headspace. It was frustrating.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts sometimes because they made me anxious. Putting my thoughts out there for others to see felt intimidating, even though I knew participating was important. I'd tell myself I'd do it later when I had something better to say, but then the deadline would sneak up on me. Fear of judgment held me back.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my LMS usage. I'd ramp up my activity in the days before something was due, logging in more frequently and spending longer sessions online. Without deadlines, I probably would have spread things out more evenly. But that pressure was what kept me accountable and made sure I actually completed things.
Q17: Feedback definitely motivated me when it was positive—it made me feel like my effort was paying off. When I got good marks, I'd feel more confident logging in and tackling the next thing. But if feedback was critical, it sometimes scared me and made me hesitant to engage right away. I needed time to process it before moving forward.
Q18: The course structure helped me because it was predictable. I knew when things were due and could plan around that. Having regular weekly modules gave me a framework to follow even when I wasn't attending class. But the structure also meant that if I fell behind, it felt overwhelming to catch up because everything was sequential.
Q19: The video lectures made engagement easier because they fit my learning style. But the heavy reading requirements made it harder—I'd get lost in long texts and lose focus. Also, having so many small activities sometimes felt overwhelming. I would have preferred fewer, more substantial tasks that I could really focus on.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for understanding but struggling to produce work. They could be watching videos, reading materials, trying to grasp concepts, but then feel too scared or overwhelmed to actually submit something. The activity shows effort, but the fear of failure might be blocking the final step of turning something in.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me in some ways. I didn't always show high activity because I studied offline so much, but I still performed decently. If you know how to learn efficiently and you use external resources, you don't need to be constantly logged in. The LMS is just one tool, not the whole picture of learning.
Q22: A course log can show patterns—when you're active, how often you engage, what you click on. But it misses the quality of that engagement and all the learning that happens away from the screen. It can't capture the fear I felt, the conversations I had with friends, or the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures. It's just surface-level data.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm actively thinking, questioning, and trying to connect new information to what I already know. For me, it's also about feeling emotionally invested—wanting to do well because the content matters to me, not just because I need the grade.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I felt sometimes, or how much effort it took to push through that fear. It wouldn't capture the CBT session I had that helped me manage my stress, or the way I'd talk myself through anxiety before starting an assignment. My emotional journey through this course is completely invisible in the data, but it shaped everything.
